documents.text: For twenty-five years, Ananda served as the Buddha's personal attendant, memorizing all his discourses and attending to his every daily need with unmatched devotion.
documents.source_name: Buddhist Suttas (Sacred Books of the East, Vol. 11), translated by T. W. Rhys Davids (1881)

documents.text: Ananda pleaded with the Buddha to allow women to enter the Sangha, leading to the ordination of Mahapajapati Gotami and the establishment of the Bhikkhuni order.
documents.source_name: Buddhist Suttas (Sacred Books of the East, Vol. 11), translated by T. W. Rhys Davids (1881)

documents.text: When Nalagiri, a wild elephant, was set loose to kill the Buddha, Ananda stepped in front of the Buddha to protect him with his own body, ignoring personal safety.
documents.source_name: Buddhist Suttas (Sacred Books of the East, Vol. 11), translated by T. W. Rhys Davids (1881)

documents.text: Ananda went into the monastery, and stood leaning against the lintel of the door, weeping at the thought: "Alas! I remain still but a learner, one who has yet to work out his own perfection. And the Master is about to pass away!"
documents.source_name: Buddhist Suttas (Sacred Books of the East, Vol. 11), translated by T. W. Rhys Davids (1881)

documents.text: The Blessed One comforted his disciple: "Enough, Ananda! Do not let yourself be troubled; do not weep! Have I not told you that it is in the very nature of things that we must divide ourselves from all things near and dear?"
documents.source_name: Buddhist Suttas (Sacred Books of the East, Vol. 11), translated by T. W. Rhys Davids (1881)

documents.text: At the First Council, Ananda began every discourse with "Thus have I heard" (Evam me sutam), reciting the Suttas from memory to preserve them for future generations.
documents.source_name: Buddhist Suttas (Sacred Books of the East, Vol. 11), translated by T. W. Rhys Davids (1881)

documents.text: Ananda achieved complete enlightenment (Arhatship) just before the First Buddhist Council opened, earning the praise of all senior monks for his humility, service, and wisdom.
documents.source_name: Buddhist Suttas (Sacred Books of the East, Vol. 11), translated by T. W. Rhys Davids (1881)

documents.text: This entry compiles the historical accounts and translations from T. W. Rhys Davids' "Buddhist Suttas" (Sacred Books of the East, Vol. 11, 1881), which is in the public domain.
documents.source_name: Buddhist Suttas (Sacred Books of the East, Vol. 11), translated by T. W. Rhys Davids (1881)

documents.text: Arjuna appears in this passage of the Mahabharata under several epithets used by the narrator: "the diadem-decked (Arjuna)", "the ape-bannered Arjuna" (from the emblem of Hanuman on his banner), "Phalguni", "Dhananjaya the son of Pandu", "Vibhatsu", and "the wielder of Gandiva" (his bow), "that chastiser of foes...who was capable of drawing the bow with even his left hand."
documents.source_name: The Mahabharata, Book 6 (Bhishma Parva), translated by Kisari Mohan Ganguli (1883-1896)

documents.text: Krishna, "cheerfully addressing Dhananjaya," told Arjuna: "There, Bhishma, the son of Santanu, stayeth between the two armies. Slaying him by putting forth thy might, thou mayst win victory. There, at that spot, whence he breaketh our ranks, check him, putting forth thy strength." "Thus urged, the ape-bannered Arjuna at that moment made Bhishma with his car, steeds, and standard, invisible by means of his arrows."
documents.source_name: The Mahabharata, Book 6 (Bhishma Parva), translated by Kisari Mohan Ganguli (1883-1896)

documents.text: Because Bhishma had vowed not to raise weapons against a warrior who had formerly been born a woman, Arjuna fought from behind Sikhandin: "Sikhandin, however, that foremost of car-warriors, protected in the battle by the diadem-decked (Arjuna)..." and again, "the diadem-decked (Arjuna), excited with rage and placing Sikhandin to the fore, approached Bhishma (nearer)." The text presents this as a considered battlefield tactic against a vow, not an act the narrator condemns or praises outright — later Mahabharata tradition treats it as morally difficult.
documents.source_name: The Mahabharata, Book 6 (Bhishma Parva), translated by Kisari Mohan Ganguli (1883-1896)

documents.text: "Then Vibhatsu, drawing his bow Gandiva with a laugh, pierced the son of Ganga with five and twenty arrows. And once more, Dhananjaya, with great speed and excited with wrath struck him in every vital part with hundreds of arrows...the son of Pandu cut off in that battle even all the bows of Bhishma" — cutting three successive bows from Bhishma's hands with precisely aimed arrows rather than killing him outright.
documents.source_name: The Mahabharata, Book 6 (Bhishma Parva), translated by Kisari Mohan Ganguli (1883-1896)

documents.text: Bhishma himself testifies to Arjuna's unmatched skill even while dying from his wounds: "He is incapable of being vanquished in battle by the wielder of the thunder-bolt himself. As regards myself also, O hero, the very gods, Danavas and Rakshasas united together, are incapable of vanquishing me. What I shall say then of mighty car-warriors among men?" — acknowledging Arjuna as the one warrior capable of doing what was done.
documents.source_name: The Mahabharata, Book 6 (Bhishma Parva), translated by Kisari Mohan Ganguli (1883-1896)

documents.text: This same Book of the Mahabharata (the Bhishma Parva) also contains the Bhagavad Gita, Krishna's counsel to a hesitant Arjuna just before this battle begins, on duty, grief, and right action. The Gita is treated as a separate, already-sourced corpus in this app (the Annie Besant public domain translation) and is not reproduced here; this entry covers only Arjuna's role in the battle narrative that follows it.
documents.source_name: The Mahabharata, Book 6 (Bhishma Parva), translated by Kisari Mohan Ganguli (1883-1896)

documents.text: After Bhishma fell, "the heroic Pandavas possessed of massive arms that looked like spiked maces, having obtained the victory and won a highly blessed state in the other world, all blew their great conches." The text records mixed reactions across both armies — grief, shouting, and reflection — rather than simple celebration; Bhishma himself continued to be honored by both sides even after his fall.
documents.source_name: The Mahabharata, Book 6 (Bhishma Parva), translated by Kisari Mohan Ganguli (1883-1896)

documents.text: Arjuna (Dhananjaya, Phalguni, Vibhatsu), third of the Pandava brothers, is a central figure of the Mahabharata's Bhishma Parva (Book 6). This entry draws only on Sections CXIX-CXX of the Ganguli translation (1883-1896), public domain, digitized jointly by sacred-texts.com and Project Gutenberg. It does not cover the Bhagavad Gita itself, which is a separate already-sourced corpus, nor other Parvas of the epic.
documents.source_name: The Mahabharata, Book 6 (Bhishma Parva), translated by Kisari Mohan Ganguli (1883-1896)

documents.text: "...thy sire Bhishma, influenced by wrath, began to fight with the Srinjayas...the old Kuru grandsire Bhishma, day after day, slew ten thousand warriors of the Ratha. On the tenth day, however...Bhishma, single-handed, slew ten thousand elephants...none of the kings, O monarch, could even look at him, for he then resembled the hot mid-day Sun in the summer sky."
documents.source_name: The Mahabharata, Book 6 (Bhishma Parva), translated by Kisari Mohan Ganguli (1883-1896)

documents.text: Krishna, seeing Bhishma break the Pandava ranks, said to Arjuna: "There, Bhishma, the son of Santanu, stayeth between the two armies. Slaying him by putting forth thy might, thou mayst win victory." Bhishma fought on the Kaurava side out of a prior vow of loyalty to the throne of Hastinapura, not from personal enmity toward the Pandavas, whom the text elsewhere calls his own kin.
documents.source_name: The Mahabharata, Book 6 (Bhishma Parva), translated by Kisari Mohan Ganguli (1883-1896)

documents.text: "...he continued...'With only a single bow I could slay all the Pandavas, if the mighty Vishnu himself had not been their protector. For two reasons, however, I will not fight with the Pandavas, viz., their unslayableness, and the femininity of Sikhandin... I should now wish my own death, this being the proper hour.'" Bhishma restrained his own full strength against the Pandavas by choice, even while continuing to fight for the Kaurava army.
documents.source_name: The Mahabharata, Book 6 (Bhishma Parva), translated by Kisari Mohan Ganguli (1883-1896)

documents.text: "...he fell down from his car with his head to the east, a little before sunset...That foremost of all bowmen, that mighty-armed hero, fell down, like an uprooted standard of Indra, making the earth tremble the while. Pierced all over with arrows, his body touched not the ground. At that moment...a divine nature took possession of that great bowman lying on a bed of arrows."
documents.source_name: The Mahabharata, Book 6 (Bhishma Parva), translated by Kisari Mohan Ganguli (1883-1896)

documents.text: "The boon that was granted to me by my illustrious sire, to the effect that my death would depend on my own wish O, let that boon become true. I will hold my life, since I have control in the matter of laying it down... I will never pass out (of the world) as long as the Sun is in the southern solstice... I will proceed to my own ancient abode when the Sun reacheth the northern solstice." Bhishma lay on his bed of arrows for many weeks, by his own account, until the turning of the season, so as to die at an auspicious time.
documents.source_name: The Mahabharata, Book 6 (Bhishma Parva), translated by Kisari Mohan Ganguli (1883-1896)

documents.text: Of the arrows piercing him, Bhishma told Dussasana: "These arrows coursing towards me in one continuous line, whose touch resembleth that of heaven's bolt, have been shot by Arjuna. These are not Sikhandin's... Save the heroic wielder of Gandiva, viz., the ape-bannered Jishnu, even all other kings united together cannot cause me pain." Even mortally wounded, Bhishma names and honors the skill of the warrior who defeated him rather than complaining of his fate.
documents.source_name: The Mahabharata, Book 6 (Bhishma Parva), translated by Kisari Mohan Ganguli (1883-1896)

documents.text: "When the grandsire of the Bharatas who was endued with great might was overthrown, thy son...knew not what to do. And all the Kurus were entirely deprived of their senses... And the Rishis and the Pitris all applauded Bhishma of high vows. And the deceased ancestors of the Bharatas also praised Bhishma." Bhishma, endued with great ascetic merit, "having recourse to that Yoga which is taught in the great Upanishads and engaged in mental prayers, remained quiet, expectant of his hour" even after his fall.
documents.source_name: The Mahabharata, Book 6 (Bhishma Parva), translated by Kisari Mohan Ganguli (1883-1896)

documents.text: Bhishma (Devavrata, son of Ganga and Santanu) is a central figure of the Mahabharata's Bhishma Parva (Book 6), which also contains the Bhagavad Gita (a separate, already-sourced corpus in this app). This entry draws only on Sections CXIX-CXX of the Ganguli translation (1883-1896), public domain, digitized jointly by sacred-texts.com and Project Gutenberg. It does not cover Bhishma's earlier vow of lifelong celibacy (Adi Parva, Book 1), which is not yet sourced for this app.
documents.source_name: The Mahabharata, Book 6 (Bhishma Parva), translated by Kisari Mohan Ganguli (1883-1896)

documents.text: Chanakya (Kautilya) was a teacher at Taxila and counselor to Chandragupta Maurya, seeking to unite the divided kingdoms of India under a single righteous administration.
documents.source_name: Kautilya's Arthashastra, translated by R. Shamasastry (1915)

documents.text: After being insulted by the Nanda king, Chanakya untied his topknot (shikha), vowing to keep it untied until he had destroyed the corrupt dynasty and established a just rule under Chandragupta.
documents.source_name: Kautilya's Arthashastra, translated by R. Shamasastry (1915)

documents.text: Chanakya selected the young Chandragupta, trained him in martial arts and statecraft, and navigated immense political intrigues to guide him to the Maurya throne.
documents.source_name: Kautilya's Arthashastra, translated by R. Shamasastry (1915)

documents.text: Though serving as the prime minister of a vast empire, Chanakya lived in a humble mud hut, stating that a ruler's wealth lies in the prosperity of his subjects, not in personal luxury.
documents.source_name: Kautilya's Arthashastra, translated by R. Shamasastry (1915)

documents.text: "In the happiness of his subjects lies the king's happiness; in their welfare his welfare. What pleases himself he shall not consider as good, but whatever pleases his subjects he shall consider as good." (Rajadharma, Book I, Chapter XIX).
documents.source_name: Kautilya's Arthashastra, translated by R. Shamasastry (1915)

documents.text: "Just as it is impossible not to taste honey or poison that finds itself on the tongue, so it is impossible for a government servant not to eat up at least a bit of the king's revenue," advising strict accountability.
documents.source_name: Kautilya's Arthashastra, translated by R. Shamasastry (1915)

documents.text: Chanakya's guidance led to the creation of the Maurya Empire, which brought peace, unity, and prosperity across the subcontinent, and his writings remain a classic on ethical governance.
documents.source_name: Kautilya's Arthashastra, translated by R. Shamasastry (1915)

documents.text: This entry compiles the translation from R. Shamasastry's "Kautilya's Arthashastra" (1915), which is in the public domain.
documents.source_name: Kautilya's Arthashastra, translated by R. Shamasastry (1915)

documents.text: Ashoka inherited the Maurya Empire from his father Bindusara, ruling a vast territory with iron authority before his transformation and dedication to the path of Dharma.
documents.source_name: Asoka, the Buddhist Emperor of India, by Vincent A. Smith (1901)

documents.text: Directly after the annexation of Kalinga, His Sacred Majesty felt remorse; for the conquest of an unconquered country involves slaughter, death, and captivity of the people, leading Ashoka to embrace Buddhism.
documents.source_name: Asoka, the Buddhist Emperor of India, by Vincent A. Smith (1901)

documents.text: Ashoka renounced armed conquest (Digvijaya), replacing it with conquest by Dharma (Dharmavijaya), and dedicating imperial resources to public welfare, hospitals, and animal protection.
documents.source_name: Asoka, the Buddhist Emperor of India, by Vincent A. Smith (1901)

documents.text: Ashoka sent his son Mahendra and daughter Sanghamitra to Sri Lanka as Buddhist monastics to spread the message of peace, sacrificing their companionship for the growth of the Sangha.
documents.source_name: Asoka, the Buddhist Emperor of India, by Vincent A. Smith (1901)

documents.text: The Emperor undertook long pilgrimages to holy sites like Lumbini and Bodh Gaya, replacing royal hunts with tours of piety, and living a disciplined, simple life dedicated to moral uplift.
documents.source_name: Asoka, the Buddhist Emperor of India, by Vincent A. Smith (1901)

documents.text: "A man must not do reverence to his own sect by disparaging that of another... concord is meritorious, that they should hear and respect each other's Dharma." (Rock Edict XII on tolerance).
documents.source_name: Asoka, the Buddhist Emperor of India, by Vincent A. Smith (1901)

documents.text: Ashoka's edicts inscribed on pillars and rocks across Asia established a legacy of state-sponsored compassion, religious harmony, and ethical governance that influenced global history.
documents.source_name: Asoka, the Buddhist Emperor of India, by Vincent A. Smith (1901)

documents.text: This entry compiles the historical translation of the Rock Edicts from Vincent A. Smith's "Asoka, the Buddhist Emperor of India" (1901), which is in the public domain.
documents.source_name: Asoka, the Buddhist Emperor of India, by Vincent A. Smith (1901)

documents.text: "The Guru, on seeing his son, felt his end was near, and having placed the coconut and five paisa before him, bowed to him in token of his succession." Born in Patna, Gobind Rai succeeded his father Guru Tegh Bahadur at a young age to lead the Sikhs.
documents.source_name: The Sikh Religion, Vol. 5, translated by Max Arthur Macauliffe (1909)

documents.text: "The Guru arose and said: 'Is there any Sikh of mine who will offer his head to my sword?' ... When five Sikhs had thus offered their lives, he baptized them with Amrit (holy nectar) and declared them the Panj Pyare (the Five Beloved Ones), initiating the Khalsa in 1699."
documents.source_name: The Sikh Religion, Vol. 5, translated by Max Arthur Macauliffe (1909)

documents.text: The Guru's family and disciples faced extreme hunger and cold during the prolonged siege of Anandpur. Yet the Guru refused to surrender, reminding them that the path of Dharma requires enduring all trials.
documents.source_name: The Sikh Religion, Vol. 5, translated by Max Arthur Macauliffe (1909)

documents.text: Ajit Singh and Jujhar Singh, the Guru's eldest sons, fell fighting bravely against overwhelming forces at Chamkaur. The Guru watched them go to battle with joy, stating: "O God, it is You who gave them to me, and I return them to You in battle for Dharma."
documents.source_name: The Sikh Religion, Vol. 5, translated by Max Arthur Macauliffe (1909)

documents.text: The two youngest sons, Zorawar Singh and Fateh Singh, were bricked up alive at Sirhind for refusing to renounce their faith. They stated: "Our grandfather Guru Tegh Bahadur gave his life but not his faith; we shall follow his path."
documents.source_name: The Sikh Religion, Vol. 5, translated by Max Arthur Macauliffe (1909)

documents.text: "When all other means have failed, it is righteous to draw the sword." In his letter (Zafarnama) to Aurangzeb, the Guru declared that he did not fear imperial armies, for his trust was in the Lord of the universe.
documents.source_name: The Sikh Religion, Vol. 5, translated by Max Arthur Macauliffe (1909)

documents.text: Before passing, the Guru placed the coconut and five paisa before the Adi Granth, bowed, and said: "Let him who desires to behold me look into the Granth Sahib. Obey the Granth Sahib as the visible body of the Gurus."
documents.source_name: The Sikh Religion, Vol. 5, translated by Max Arthur Macauliffe (1909)

documents.text: This entry compiles the historical accounts and translations from Max Arthur Macauliffe's "The Sikh Religion", Vol. 5 (1909), which is in the public domain and hosted on sacred-texts.com.
documents.source_name: The Sikh Religion, Vol. 5, translated by Max Arthur Macauliffe (1909)

documents.text: Born in Talwandi, Guru Nanak from his childhood was of a contemplative mind, preferring spiritual discussions to trade or agriculture, and refusing to wear the ritual thread, seeking instead the inner thread of love and truth.
documents.source_name: The Sikh Religion, Vol. 1, translated by Max Arthur Macauliffe (1909)

documents.text: After emerging from three days in the Bein River, Guru Nanak declared: "There is no Hindu, and there is no Musalman," asserting the unity of humanity under one Creator and initiating his mission.
documents.source_name: The Sikh Religion, Vol. 1, translated by Max Arthur Macauliffe (1909)

documents.text: Guru Nanak walked thousands of miles across India, Mecca, and Tibet (in journeys known as Udasis), facing hostile crowds, wild beasts, and imprisonment to spread the message of truth and devotion.
documents.source_name: The Sikh Religion, Vol. 1, translated by Max Arthur Macauliffe (1909)

documents.text: Confronting the robber Sajjan, who murdered travelers, the Guru sang a hymn about deceitful lives, causing Sajjan to repent, give up his wealth, and build the first dharamsala (house of worship).
documents.source_name: The Sikh Religion, Vol. 1, translated by Max Arthur Macauliffe (1909)

documents.text: Imprisoned by Babur's invading army, Guru Nanak's spiritual poise impressed the Emperor, who released the prisoners and sought the Guru's blessing, recognizing his divine authority.
documents.source_name: The Sikh Religion, Vol. 1, translated by Max Arthur Macauliffe (1909)

documents.text: Guru Nanak established the three pillars of Sikh life: earn an honest living (Kirat Karo), remember the Divine Name (Naam Japna), and share your earnings with the needy (Vand Chhako).
documents.source_name: The Sikh Religion, Vol. 1, translated by Max Arthur Macauliffe (1909)

documents.text: Establishing a community of farmers at Kartarpur where all dined together in a common kitchen (Langar), the Guru chose his devoted disciple Bhai Lehna (Guru Angad) as his successor, passing his light to him.
documents.source_name: The Sikh Religion, Vol. 1, translated by Max Arthur Macauliffe (1909)

documents.text: This entry compiles the historical accounts and translations from Max Arthur Macauliffe's "The Sikh Religion", Vol. 1 (1909), which is in the public domain and hosted on sacred-texts.com.
documents.source_name: The Sikh Religion, Vol. 1, translated by Max Arthur Macauliffe (1909)

documents.text: Tegh Bahadur spent twenty years in quiet contemplation in a cellar at Bakala, until he was revealed to the public by Makhan Shah Lubana who cried, "Guru Ladho re" (The Guru has been found), acknowledging his spiritual path.
documents.source_name: The Sikh Religion, Vol. 4, translated by Max Arthur Macauliffe (1909)

documents.text: When Kashmiri Pandits sought the Guru's protection from forced conversion, the Guru told them: "Tell the Emperor that if he converts Guru Tegh Bahadur, you will all follow him," defending their freedom of religion.
documents.source_name: The Sikh Religion, Vol. 4, translated by Max Arthur Macauliffe (1909)

documents.text: Knowing the certainty of death, the Guru traveled to Delhi to meet the Emperor, offering himself as a sacrifice to protect the liberty of conscience of another faith.
documents.source_name: The Sikh Religion, Vol. 4, translated by Max Arthur Macauliffe (1909)

documents.text: The Guru's close disciples Bhai Mati Das (sawn alive), Bhai Sati Das (burnt alive), and Bhai Dayala (boiled in water) were executed before him, yet they all died with the Guru's hymns on their lips.
documents.source_name: The Sikh Religion, Vol. 4, translated by Max Arthur Macauliffe (1909)

documents.text: Refusing to show a miracle or convert, the Guru was beheaded in Delhi in 1675, showing that life is fleeting but truth and honor are eternal.
documents.source_name: The Sikh Religion, Vol. 4, translated by Max Arthur Macauliffe (1909)

documents.text: In his hymns of detachment (Salok Mahalla 9), the Guru taught: "Fear not, nor frighten anyone" (Bhai Kahu Kau Det Neh, Neh Bhai Manat Aan), as the key to spiritual liberation.
documents.source_name: The Sikh Religion, Vol. 4, translated by Max Arthur Macauliffe (1909)

documents.text: The sacrifice of Guru Tegh Bahadur galvanized the Sikh community, leading his son Guru Gobind Singh to establish the Khalsa to fight oppression and secure human rights.
documents.source_name: The Sikh Religion, Vol. 4, translated by Max Arthur Macauliffe (1909)

documents.text: This entry compiles the historical accounts and translations from Max Arthur Macauliffe's "The Sikh Religion", Vol. 4 (1909), which is in the public domain and hosted on sacred-texts.com.
documents.source_name: The Sikh Religion, Vol. 4, translated by Max Arthur Macauliffe (1909)

documents.text: Harishchandra ruled Ayodhya with justice and piety, having vowed that he would never speak a lie under any circumstances, gaining reknown for his truthfulness.
documents.source_name: The Markandeya Purana, translated by F. Eden Pargiter (1904)

documents.text: To fulfill a promise made in a dream and tested by Sage Vishvamitra, Harishchandra gladly gave away his kingdom, wealth, and empire, walking out as a pauper to preserve his integrity.
documents.source_name: The Markandeya Purana, translated by F. Eden Pargiter (1904)

documents.text: To pay the ritual fee (dakshina) demanded by the sage, Harishchandra sold his wife Shaivya and young son Rohitashva into slavery to a brahmin, bearing the pain of separation.
documents.source_name: The Markandeya Purana, translated by F. Eden Pargiter (1904)

documents.text: To pay the remaining fee, the King sold himself to an outcaste who managed the cremation grounds, performing the menial duty of collecting shroud-fees from the dead.
documents.source_name: The Markandeya Purana, translated by F. Eden Pargiter (1904)

documents.text: When his son died of a snakebite and Shaivya brought the body for cremation, Harishchandra refused to burn the body without the standard fee, prioritizing his duty and truth over personal grief.
documents.source_name: The Markandeya Purana, translated by F. Eden Pargiter (1904)

documents.text: Harishchandra taught that Dharma is rooted in Truth. A kingdom may fall and life may perish, but Truth must never be abandoned under any pressure or trial.
documents.source_name: The Markandeya Purana, translated by F. Eden Pargiter (1904)

documents.text: Impressed by his absolute integrity, the gods appeared, restored his son to life, and invited Harishchandra and his loyal subjects to ascend to the heavens in glory.
documents.source_name: The Markandeya Purana, translated by F. Eden Pargiter (1904)

documents.text: This entry compiles the translation from F. Eden Pargiter's "The Markandeya Purana" (1904), published by the Asiatic Society of Bengal, which is in the public domain.
documents.source_name: The Markandeya Purana, translated by F. Eden Pargiter (1904)

documents.text: "...the Venerable Ascetic Mahavira, the last of the Tirthakaras...took the form of an embryo in the womb of Devananda...in the brahmanical part of the town Kundagrama." By the god Sakra's order, the embryo was then transferred to the womb of "the Kshatriyani Trisala...wife of the Kshatriya Siddhartha," since it is held that Arhats are never born from a brahmanical womb, only a noble Kshatriya one.
documents.source_name: Jaina Sutras, Part I (Sacred Books of the East, Vol. 22), translated by Hermann Jacobi (1884), Kalpa Sutra: Life of Mahavira

documents.text: "The Venerable Ascetic Mahavira belonged to the Kasyapa gotra. His three names have thus been recorded: by his parents he was called Vardhamana; because he is devoid of love and hate, he is called Sramana (i.e. Ascetic); because he stands fast in midst of dangers and fears, patiently bears hardships and calamities, adheres to the chosen rules of penance, is wise, indifferent to pleasure and pain, rich in control, and gifted with fortitude, the name Venerable Ascetic Mahavira has been given him by the gods."
documents.source_name: Jaina Sutras, Part I (Sacred Books of the East, Vol. 22), translated by Hermann Jacobi (1884), Kalpa Sutra: Life of Mahavira

documents.text: At age thirty, after his parents' death, Mahavira renounced the world: "He left his silver, he left his gold, he left his riches, corn, majesty, and kingdom...he distributed presents through proper persons, he distributed presents among indigent persons." He then "descended from his palankin, took off his ornaments, garlands, and finery with his own hands, and with his own hands plucked out his hair in five handfuls," fasting two and a half days before entering "the state of houselessness."
documents.source_name: Jaina Sutras, Part I (Sacred Books of the East, Vol. 22), translated by Hermann Jacobi (1884), Kalpa Sutra: Life of Mahavira

documents.text: "The Venerable Ascetic Mahavira for a year and a month wore clothes; after that time he walked about naked, and accepted the alms in the hollow of his hand. For more than twelve years the Venerable Ascetic Mahavira neglected his body and abandoned the care of it; he with equanimity bore, underwent, and suffered all pleasant or unpleasant occurrences arising from divine powers, men, or animals."
documents.source_name: Jaina Sutras, Part I (Sacred Books of the East, Vol. 22), translated by Hermann Jacobi (1884), Kalpa Sutra: Life of Mahavira

documents.text: Of his equanimity through those years of austerity: "his heart was pure like the water...in autumn; nothing could soil him like the leaf of a lotus; his senses were well protected like those of a tortoise; he was single and alone like the horn of a rhinoceros; he was free like a bird...strong like a bull, difficult to attack like a lion, steady and firm like Mount Mandara, deep like the ocean, mild like the moon...pure like excellent gold...like a well-kindled fire he shone in his splendour."
documents.source_name: Jaina Sutras, Part I (Sacred Books of the East, Vol. 22), translated by Hermann Jacobi (1884), Kalpa Sutra: Life of Mahavira

documents.text: After thirteen years of ascetic practice, "outside of the town Grimbhikagrama on the bank of the river Rigupalika...under a Sal tree...in a squatting position with joined heels, exposing himself to the heat of the sun, after fasting two and a half days without drinking water, being engaged in deep meditation, (he) reached the highest knowledge and intuition, called Kevala, which is infinite, supreme, unobstructed, unimpeded, complete, and full." As a Kevalin, the text says he then "knew and saw all conditions of the world."
documents.source_name: Jaina Sutras, Part I (Sacred Books of the East, Vol. 22), translated by Hermann Jacobi (1884), Kalpa Sutra: Life of Mahavira

documents.text: Mahavira taught for thirty more years before dying at Pava. The text records his community at the time of his death as "an excellent community of fourteen thousand Sramanas...thirty-six thousand nuns...one hundred and fifty-nine thousand lay votaries...three hundred and eighteen thousand female lay votaries," among other named ranks of advanced disciples — the fourfold Jain community (monks, nuns, laymen, laywomen) that continues today.
documents.source_name: Jaina Sutras, Part I (Sacred Books of the East, Vol. 22), translated by Hermann Jacobi (1884), Kalpa Sutra: Life of Mahavira

documents.text: Mahavira (Vardhamana), the 24th and last Tirthakara of this era in Jain tradition, is the subject of the Kalpa Sutra's Life of Mahavira (Lectures 1-5). This entry draws only on Lecture 2 (conception) and Lecture 5 (naming, renunciation, austerities, enlightenment, death, community) of Hermann Jacobi's 1884 translation, Sacred Books of the East vol. 22, public domain. The Kalpa Sutra's earlier lectures and its separate Life of Parsva are not reproduced here.
documents.source_name: Jaina Sutras, Part I (Sacred Books of the East, Vol. 22), translated by Hermann Jacobi (1884), Kalpa Sutra: Life of Mahavira

documents.text: The Arhat Parsva was born in Varanasi as the son of King Asvasena and Queen Vama, living 250 years before the nirvana of Mahavira as a prince of noble character.
documents.source_name: Jaina Sutras, Part I (Sacred Books of the East, Vol. 22), translated by Hermann Jacobi (1884), Kalpa Sutra: Life of Parsva

documents.text: Parsva renounced his royal life to establish the four great vows (Chaturyama Dharma): non-violence (ahimsa), truthfulness (satya), non-stealing (asteya), and non-possession (aparigraha).
documents.source_name: Jaina Sutras, Part I (Sacred Books of the East, Vol. 22), translated by Hermann Jacobi (1884), Kalpa Sutra: Life of Parsva

documents.text: While Parsva sat in deep meditation, the demon Kamatha (his enemy from past lives) raised a terrifying storm, hurling rain, winds, and stones to break his spiritual resolve.
documents.source_name: Jaina Sutras, Part I (Sacred Books of the East, Vol. 22), translated by Hermann Jacobi (1884), Kalpa Sutra: Life of Parsva

documents.text: The serpent king Dharanendra rose from the earth and spread his seven hoods over Parsva's head like an umbrella, protecting him from the fury of the storm.
documents.source_name: Jaina Sutras, Part I (Sacred Books of the East, Vol. 22), translated by Hermann Jacobi (1884), Kalpa Sutra: Life of Parsva

documents.text: Throughout the storm and the protection, Parsva remained completely detached, feeling neither anger towards the demon nor attachment to the protector, maintaining perfect equanimity.
documents.source_name: Jaina Sutras, Part I (Sacred Books of the East, Vol. 22), translated by Hermann Jacobi (1884), Kalpa Sutra: Life of Parsva

documents.text: Parsva taught that all souls are equal and that the highest spiritual duty is the absolute avoidance of harm to any living being, visible or invisible, establishing the foundation of Jain ethics.
documents.source_name: Jaina Sutras, Part I (Sacred Books of the East, Vol. 22), translated by Hermann Jacobi (1884), Kalpa Sutra: Life of Parsva

documents.text: After seventy years of teaching, Parsva achieved ultimate liberation (Nirvana) on Mount Sammed Shikhar, which remains today the most sacred pilgrimage site for Jains.
documents.source_name: Jaina Sutras, Part I (Sacred Books of the East, Vol. 22), translated by Hermann Jacobi (1884), Kalpa Sutra: Life of Parsva

documents.text: This entry compiles the translation from Hermann Jacobi's "Jaina Sutras, Part I" (Sacred Books of the East, Vol. 22, 1884), Kalpa Sutra: Life of Parsva, which is in the public domain.
documents.source_name: Jaina Sutras, Part I (Sacred Books of the East, Vol. 22), translated by Hermann Jacobi (1884), Kalpa Sutra: Life of Parsva

documents.text: Shivaji's character was shaped by his mother Jijabai, who taught him stories of ancient heroes, and Dadoji Kondadev, who trained him in administration and military tactics in the rugged valleys of Maval.
documents.source_name: Shivaji and His Times, by Jadunath Sarkar (1919)

documents.text: Shivaji's goal was not merely personal kingdom but the establishment of a self-governing state where all people could practice their faith without fear of oppression, known as Hindavi Swarajya (self-rule).
documents.source_name: Shivaji and His Times, by Jadunath Sarkar (1919)

documents.text: When Adil Shahi general Afzal Khan marched with a massive army to crush the Marathas, Shivaji met him in a personal interview under Pratapgad, successfully defending himself and defeating the larger force through strategic foresight.
documents.source_name: Shivaji and His Times, by Jadunath Sarkar (1919)

documents.text: Imprisoned by Aurangzeb in Agra, Shivaji maintained his composure and escaped by concealing himself in large sweetmeat baskets, returning safely to Maharashtra to resume his struggle for independence.
documents.source_name: Shivaji and His Times, by Jadunath Sarkar (1919)

documents.text: Baji Prabhu Deshpande and a handful of soldiers held the pass of Ghodkhind (Pavankhind) to allow Shivaji to escape to Vishalgad, sacrificing their lives so their leader could survive to lead the Maratha cause.
documents.source_name: Shivaji and His Times, by Jadunath Sarkar (1919)

documents.text: Shivaji ordered his soldiers never to damage mosques, to treat women and children of all communities with respect, and to hand over any copy of the Quran found in battle to Muslim officers, instituting a model of religious tolerance.
documents.source_name: Shivaji and His Times, by Jadunath Sarkar (1919)

documents.text: Shivaji was crowned Chhatrapati at Raigad in 1674, establishing a sovereign Hindu kingdom that could challenge imperial hegemony and preserve local culture, language, and traditions.
documents.source_name: Shivaji and His Times, by Jadunath Sarkar (1919)

documents.text: This entry compiles the historical records and translations from Jadunath Sarkar's "Shivaji and His Times" (1919), which is in the public domain.
documents.source_name: Shivaji and His Times, by Jadunath Sarkar (1919)

documents.text: Siddhartha Gautama left his palace, wife, and child to seek the end of suffering, wandering as an ascetic before achieving Enlightenment under the Bodhi tree and becoming the Buddha.
documents.source_name: Buddhist Suttas (Sacred Books of the East, Vol. 11), translated by T. W. Rhys Davids (1881)

documents.text: Setting in motion the Wheel of Dharma (Dhammacakkappavattana Sutta) in his first sermon, the Buddha taught the Middle Way between self-indulgence and self-mortification, and the Four Noble Truths.
documents.source_name: Buddhist Suttas (Sacred Books of the East, Vol. 11), translated by T. W. Rhys Davids (1881)

documents.text: Before his enlightenment, the Buddha practiced extreme fasting, eating only a single grain of rice a day, until he realized that physical torture does not lead to spiritual clarity.
documents.source_name: Buddhist Suttas (Sacred Books of the East, Vol. 11), translated by T. W. Rhys Davids (1881)

documents.text: The Buddha calmly faced multiple attempts on his life by his cousin Devadatta, including a runaway wild elephant, which he tamed with loving-kindness.
documents.source_name: Buddhist Suttas (Sacred Books of the East, Vol. 11), translated by T. W. Rhys Davids (1881)

documents.text: Even when mortally ill from his last meal, the Buddha walked long distances to Kusinara, instructing his disciples to not blame the donor of the food.
documents.source_name: Buddhist Suttas (Sacred Books of the East, Vol. 11), translated by T. W. Rhys Davids (1881)

documents.text: "Therefore, O Ananda, be ye lamps unto yourselves. Be ye a refuge to yourselves. Betake yourselves to no external refuge. Hold fast to the truth as a lamp." The Buddha emphasized personal realization.
documents.source_name: Buddhist Suttas (Sacred Books of the East, Vol. 11), translated by T. W. Rhys Davids (1881)

documents.text: "Decay is inherent in all component things! Work out your salvation with diligence!" These were the last words of the Tathagata before he entered Parinirvana.
documents.source_name: Buddhist Suttas (Sacred Books of the East, Vol. 11), translated by T. W. Rhys Davids (1881)

documents.text: This entry compiles the historical accounts and translations from T. W. Rhys Davids' "Buddhist Suttas" (Sacred Books of the East, Vol. 11, 1881), which is in the public domain and hosted on sacred-texts.com.
documents.source_name: Buddhist Suttas (Sacred Books of the East, Vol. 11), translated by T. W. Rhys Davids (1881)
